page.title: Festival of the Deva and Naraka

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Festival of the Deva and Naraka]][[ja:六道冥官祭]]





{{Multiple issues|{{more footnotes|date=January 2021}}
{{Essay-like|date=January 2021}}}}
{{ill|Festival of the Deva and Naraka|ja|六道冥官祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival of the Deva and Naraka|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (六道冥官祭) is an Onmyodo festival seen as the Japanese equivalent of {{ill|Feng Shan|en|Feng Shan|qid=Q10957146|lt=Feng Shan|1=Feng Shan}}.
The {{ill|Festival of the Deva and Naraka|ja|六道冥官祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival of the Deva and Naraka|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, an onmyōdō ritual that had always been performed from the time of {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|qid=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}} (1571–1617) until the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}} (1831–1867), the last Emperor of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, was not performed for Emperor Meiji (1852–1912). The Tokugawa shoguns, like the Emperors, have always performed the festival every time they were {{ill|Shogun senge|lt=given the position of shogun by the Emperors|ja|将軍宣下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogun senge|qq=}}. The Tsuchimikado family lost their official position in charge of onmyōdō, and also lost the exclusive right to issue the license, and although they had no choice but to further transform the Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto into more shintoistically, they were deprived of their influence over private onmyōji in various regions.
==Overview==
'''Rokudō Meikan-sai''' (六道冥官祭) is one of the rituals performed in {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|qid=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}. It is also referred to as '''Tenchū Chifu-sai''' (天曺地府祭) or '''Tensō Chifu-sai''' (天曹地府祭). Within the {{ill|Abe clan|ja|阿倍氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe clan|qq=}}, which managed Onmyōdō, the character "曺" was used as a substitute for "曹." This ritual involved offerings such as gold and silver coins, fine silk, saddled horses, and items like {{ill|Namemono|ja|撫物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namemono|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, made to 12 deities centered around {{ill|Taizan Fukun|ja|泰山府君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taizan Fukun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東岳大帝|1=Dongyue Dadi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1241488}}, {{ill|Tensō|ja|天曹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Chifu|ja|地府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chifu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, to pray for good health, longevity, and safety. It was one of the most important ceremonies in Onmyōdō, often performed during national events such as the succession of emperors or {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=shōgun|1=Shogun}}.
This ritual, incorporating elements of Chinese {{ill|Feng Shan|en|Feng Shan|qid=Q10957146|lt=Feng Shan|1=Feng Shan}} ideology and Buddhist {{ill|Six Paths|en|Six Paths|qid=Q1192450|lt=Six Realms|1=Six Paths}} concepts, began during the {{ill|Insei period|ja|院政期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=Cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q865416}}. By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, it became a ceremony conducted by the Abe clan during events like the shōgun's investiture by the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}. It was also regularly or occasionally held for the emperor, {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|qid=Q134737|lt=nobles|1=Nobility}}, and {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}}, as part of prayers for disaster prevention or extraordinary celestial events.
With the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家 (安倍氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿倍氏}} becoming the main branch of the Abe clan under the patronage of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}, they took charge of performing the ritual as part of the shōgun's {{ill|Godai Hajime|ja|御代始|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godai Hajime|1=Godai Hajime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488779}} ceremony. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, this ritual was carried out not only during the shōgun's investiture but also during the {{ill|Imperial enthronement|ja|即位式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial enthronement|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 即位の礼|1=Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4202178}}, overseen by the Tsuchimikado family. The practice continued until the political turmoil during the reign of the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} led to its discontinuation, along with the abolition of the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮|1=Yin-Yang Bureau|2=ja|3=陰陽寮|4=fr|5=Bureau d'Onmyō|6=zh|7=阴阳寮|8=ko|9=음양료|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōryō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang Bureau|qq=}}.
Although an Onmyōdō ceremony, it incorporated various influences: Chinese Fengshan principles, Buddhist elements such as {{ill|Adhiṣṭhāna|en|Adhiṣṭhāna|qid=Q4682549|lt=Kaji|1=Adhiṣṭhāna}}, {{ill|Kunko|ja|燻香|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Daban|ja|打磐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daban|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; Shinto practices like {{ill|Applause|en|Applause|qid=Q814696|lt=clapping|1=Applause}}, {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11445508}}, and {{ill|Nakatomi Purification|ja|中臣祓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi Purification|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大祓詞|1=Ōharae no Kotoba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11438317}}; and {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} traditions involving instruments like the {{ill|Hōra|ja|法螺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōra|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホラガイ|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1472765}}.
The twelve deities enshrined in the ritual, known as the "Twelve Gods of the Netherworld" (冥道12神), included Taizan Fukun, Tensō, Chifu, {{ill|Suikan|ja|水官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suikan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hokutei Daio|ja|北帝大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokutei Daio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Godō Daio|ja|五道大王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Godō Daio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Shimei|ja|司命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Shiroku|ja|司禄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Rokusō Hankō|ja|六曹判官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokusō Hankō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nantō|ja|南斗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nantō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南斗六星|1=Milk Dipper|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10907953}}, {{ill|北斗|en|北斗|qid=Q18668578|lt=Hokuto|1=北斗}}, and {{ill|Kaoya Jōjin|ja|家親丈人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaoya Jōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A distinctive feature of this ritual was the use of {{ill|Saibun|ja|祭文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saibun|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17991683}} instead of the {{ill|Tojō|ja|都状|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tojō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} used in other ceremonies.
== Overview of rituals in onmyōdō ==
Among the representative rituals of onmyōdō are the Festival of the Great Emperor of the Sacred Mountain of the East, which is held to honor {{ill|Dongyue Dadi|en|Dongyue Dadi|qid=Q1241488|lt=Dongyue Dadi|1=Dongyue Dadi}}, the ruler of human life, and the Festival of the Deva and Naraka, which was held every time an Emperor ascended to the throne. The "Bunkanshō" also mentions that there were various other onmyōdō rituals. The texts of some of these rituals are still extant.
== See also ==

* [[Senji Ryakketsu]]
* [[Shikigami]]
* {{ill|Shinigami|en|Shinigami|qid=Q479033|lt=Shinigami|1=Shinigami}}

== Footnotes ==
=== Notes ===
{{NoteFoot}}
=== References ===
{{Reflist}}
== References ==
{{No footnotes|section=1|date=April 24, 2024}}
*Kosaka, Shinji. "Tenchū Chifu-sai" in ''National History Dictionary 9.'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988. ISBN 978-4-642-00509-8.
*Murayama, Shūichi. "Tenchū Chifu-sai" in ''Japanese Historical Dictionary 4.'' Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13104-8.
*Koba, Akishi. "Tensō Chifu-sai" in ''Japanese History Encyclopedia 2.'' Shogakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23002-3.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Further reading ==
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=1981 |title=日本陰陽道史総説 |trans-title=A Review of the History of Onmyōdō in Japan |url= |language=ja |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=4827310572}}
* {{cite book |last=Endō |first=Katsumi |year=1994 |title=近世陰陽道史の研究 |trans-title=Studies in the History of Early Modern Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher={{ill|Shin Jinbutsu Orai Sha|lt=Shin Jinbutsu Ōrai Sha|ja|新人物往来社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Jinbutsu Orai Sha|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501312}} |isbn=4404021569}}
* {{cite book |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |year=2004 |title=安倍晴明撰『占事略決』と陰陽道  |trans-title=Abe no Seimei's "Senji Ryakketsu" and Onmyōdō |url=http://www.kyuko.asia/book/b10889.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher={{ill|Kyuko Shoin|lt=Kyūko Shoin|ja|汲古書院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyuko Shoin|1=Kyuko Shoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552315}} |isbn=9784762941672}}
* {{cite book |last=Saitō |first=Rei |year=2007 |title=王朝時代の陰陽道 |trans-title=Onmyōdō in the Dynastic Period |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Meicho Kankō Kai |isbn=978-4839003302}}
* {{cite book |last=Yamashita |first=Katsuaki |year=1996 |title=平安時代の宗教文化と陰陽道 |trans-title=Religious Culture in the Heian Period and Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Iwata Shoin |isbn=4900697656}}
* {{cite book |last=Takahashi |first=Keiya |year=2000 |title=現代・陰陽師入門 |trans-title=Introduction to Modern Onmyōji |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Asahi Sonorama]] |isbn=4257035846}}
* {{cite book |last=Nakamura |first=Shōhachi |year=2000 |title=日本陰陽道書の研究 増補版 |trans-title=A Study of the Onmyō Books in Japan; Expanded Edition |url=http://www.kyuko.asia/book/b10888.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Kyūko Shoin |isbn=4257035846}}
* {{cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Ikkei |year=2002 |title=陰陽道 呪術と鬼神の世界 |trans-title=Onmyōdō: The World of Spells and Demons |url=https://bookclub.kodansha.co.jp/product?item=0000195204 |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Kodansha|Kōdansha]] |isbn=9784062582445}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=[[Seimei Shrine]] |first= |year=2002 |title=安倍晴明公 |trans-title=The Right Honorable Abe no Seimei |url=https://bookclub.kodansha.co.jp/product?item=0000180822 |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Kōdansha |isbn=9784062109833}}
* {{cite book |last1=Hayashi |first1=Jun |last2=Koike |first2=Jun'ichi |year=2002 |title=陰陽道の講義 |trans-title=Lecture on Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Sagano Shoin |isbn=4782303610}}
* {{cite book |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2005 |title=平安貴族と陰陽師 |trans-title=Heian Nobles and Onmyōji |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b34635.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbun Kan |isbn=4642079424}}
* {{cite book |last=Hayashi |first=Jun |year=2005 |title=近世陰陽道の研究 |trans-title=A Study of Early Modern Onmyōdō |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b32982.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbun Kan |isbn=4642034072}}
* {{cite book |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2006 |title=陰陽師 |trans-title=Onmyōji |url=http://www.chuko.co.jp/ebook/2013/07/514036.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-Shinsha]] |isbn=4121018443}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 1 古代 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 1: The Ancient Times |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017970}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 2 中世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 2: The Middle Ages |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017987}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 3 近世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 3: The Early Modern Period |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017994}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 4 特論 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 4: The Advanced Studies |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626018007}}
